segments.text: Hi everyone. Thank you so much for
coming. I'm so excited to be here at
Sonar Summit. We all had an LLM give us
confident, beautifully formatted answer
or code that was just wrong.
That happens. We have these powerful AI
systems that can process huge amounts of
information, search knowledge bases, and
generate some really great responses.
And still, we often don't get the
results that meet our expectations.
This happens not because the models
aren't smart enough, but because we need
to be much better at information design
and the infrastructure around it. AI has
given everyone speed. Code generation,
pipeline automation, agent
orchestration. What used to take weeks
now takes hours. But when everyone can
move fast, speed stops being an
advantage.
I'm Lena, senior director of developer
relations at Akamai. I help developers
and companies use AI to get effective
results through workflows they can
control. I'll be happy to connect with
you on LinkedIn.
We now live in a world of abundant AI
generated output and the question has
shifted. We're no longer asking can we
build it. We now need to ask, does what
we shipped actually do what we intended?
The companies that win the next decade
won't have the best models. They'll have
the best systems for ensuring that those
models produce reliable correct
outcomes. And today I will give you a
mental model and architectural patterns
to get there.
To understand the architectural guard
rails, we need let's take a deeper look
at philosophical and mathematical
foundation for this.
Anyone has heard of two generals
problem. Imagine two generals standing
on a hill to coordinate an attack at the
exact time or they will lose. They can
only communicate through messengers who
might get captured. Here's the dilemma.
If the first general sends attack at
5:00 a.m. message, how does he know that
the second general got the message? The
second general could send back a
messenger that he received a message.
And what if that messenger gets
captured? No matter how many messengers
they exchange, there's always one last
message that could be lost, leaving one
general uncertain.
So the two generals problem is about
uncertainty. We can't achieve 100%
alignment between human intent and AI
understanding. It's not really a bug.
It's not something that better models
will fix. It's a fundamental property of
communication itself. Your intent starts
at 100% clarity in your mind. You reduce
it to words and maybe 70% is preserved.
The AI interprets these words. Maybe 70%
of your 70% is preserved. It generates a
call with another 70% accuracy. At
every step, the translation between
internal state and external message and
interpretation of that message is an
unreliable step.
So while businesses are owned by people,
there will always be an astronomical
demand for people who can ensure the
correctness of AI results. And it's not
about whether AI can do the work. It's
about whether anyone can trust that the
work was done correctly.
Every LLM call is a non-deterministic
step. If we don't control it
structurally, it will break.
This is compounded across a system. So
agent orchestration, tool selection, RAG
retrieval, text SQL translation. Each
step introduces a failure mode. You
can't test your way into reliability
with non-deterministic systems the way
you can with deterministic code. You
actually need architectural guard rails.
So if perfect alignment is not really
possible and non-determinism is
imminent, what do we do? We don't try to
make the black box perfect. We can build
a glass box around it. And that starts
with understanding the patterns that
actually work. Whether you're coding
with AI agents or building AI into
products. The two generals problem
and non-determinism show us this
impossibility of full alignment. This is
not a bug in the model. It's a
fundamental property of communication
that shows up in real systems as a
context gap.
I worked with a team to help them fix
issues after they tried a VIP coding
strategy that went wrong. A team
replaced their ETL pipeline with AI
generated code. The transformation logic
looked elegant at first. The AI even
handled time zone conversions and null
values beautifully. What it didn't know
is that their source system APIs return
microseconds as milliseconds for records
created before a specific date. It's a
legacy quirk from some database
migration documented only in commit
messages. The senior engineer who
initially handled the edge case was
gone. Their inline comment about pre-2019
records needing a correction by a factor
of 1,000 was replaced by AI generated
documentation about timestamp
normalization.
So for 3 weeks all historical data was
imported with timestamps that were off.
The monitoring showed green because the
data was flowing and the row counts
matched. But every calculation was
corrupted. Here's what makes VIP coding
problematic. The AI generated code that
handled the happy path worked. It worked
fine in testing, but it failed in
production. Every system has
undocumented behaviors, implicit
assumptions, and edge cases that exist
nowhere in any training data. And these
aren't really bugs. They're just the
reality of systems that only engineers
who understand the full context can
handle correctly. And the context gap
shows that institutional knowledge is being lost.
So if perfect alignment is structurally
unbridgeable through prompting alone,
how do we fix it? It starts by
fundamentally changing the coding
workflow.
At the beginning, most people start with
standard prompting, Cursor VS Code,
their favorite editor. It works on
smallish tasks, but it breaks down as
complexity increases. We discovered that
endless context isn't useful. Even
million token context windows only have
a fraction of usable capacity, maybe 20
to 40% before the quality goes down. So,
you develop a pattern of starting over
when things derail. This helps, but we
need a more structured approach. We
notice that when we restart, there is no
persistent plan. We're rebuilding
context every single time. This means we
need some mechanism for continuity of
work for a structured memory. What we've
tried, what worked, and what didn't. So,
we try a slightly improved approach,
spec-driven development. We write
detailed requirement specifications and
rules and markdown files for to-do lists
and progress tracking. This is better.
You have a single source of truth
persisting across sessions. But specs
become monolithic and hard to manage at
scale, especially when we discover
incorrect functionality in the code. So
we try to chase down bugs in the code
but we discover we need a new workflow
for correcting the output. Think about
our traditional process. We write code,
it generates compiled artifacts. Those
artifacts execute. When we had a bug in
the code, we wouldn't fix compiled
binaries, right? We would fix the code
and rerun the app. In our new world, we
write specifications in English. AI
generates the code and the code becomes
our application. So now we shouldn't
always manually fix the AI code. We
should go back, fix our specification
and rerun it. This works. It works on
complex code bases. Works on solving
hard problems. But then we hit another
wall.
We discovered that reviewing AI
generated PRs, especially the huge ones,
is very hard. If productivity is so
high, you physically cannot review all
the code and when reviewing becomes the
bottleneck, it's time to rethink your
process. If we think about it, a
successful code review helps us uncover
any mental disconnects. We come to a
mental alignment and the technical
adjustment in the code follows.
This gives us our next big insight.
Plans are more reviewable than code.
With the speed of AI, we should move the
process of alignment to a higher level
to the spec and the plan. When we catch
errors at the planning level, we prevent
10 times more errors that we would
otherwise see at the implementation
level. And this leads us to two more
advanced patterns. The first one is
phased development.
We don't just write a requirements doc
and send our agent on a mission. We
start with a brainstorming doc, then a
structured plan, then split that plan
into bite-sized tasks, and finally
execute.
At each stage, we review the artifact
before giving it to the next stage. You
see here how a mistake in the research
state propagates with a much greater
impact to mistakes in the planning and
implementation.
We also use sub agents to parallelize
work. This keeps the context window for
each agent focused and helps the main
agent coordinate without polluting its
own context. But this too has a failure
point.
Keeping progress and coordination in
markdown works under light usage. We
scale it up. Markdown quickly becomes a
mess. Information gets stale.
Dependencies aren't tracked. The work
gets duplicated. So how do we actually
do this as humans? We coordinate, we
delegate, we track progress, we use
issue trackers, with dependency graphs,
with version control. Everything
AI agents kind of need the same. We're
starting to see new approaches for this.
One of them is graph-based issue
tracking. A new tool called Beads,
for example, it allows agents to manage
tasks on a graph while tracking
dependencies. So, it's a graph-based
Git-backed issue tracker database to let
coding agents track all your work.
Another one is MCP Agent Mail for multi-
agent coordination. Agents can send
messages and monitor the inbox for
updates from other agents and can
coordinate between each other on the
progress between tasks, dividing the
work and avoiding conflicts.
So, now let's get into the wild
territory.
This is Ralph Wiggum, the Simpsons
character, but also Ralph is the
autonomous AI coding loop that ships
features while you sleep. Each iteration
starts with a fresh context window and
memory persists through Git history and
text files.
It is literally a bash loop that pipes a
prompt into your agent. Agent picks the
next story from your PRD document. It
implements it. It runs type check plus
tests. It commits if everything is
passing and marks story as done. Also
logs learnings. And it loops on repeat
until it's done. It's great for
well-defined tasks, but not to be used
for ambiguous or exploratory work in
cases where you don't have clear
criteria, security critical code, and
anything that needs a human review. The
lesson from Ralph is that we use phased
implementation of a human reviewed plan
with a separate context window for
phase.
So Ralph gives you a single tireless
agent. But what if you need a whole
team?
This is the evolution of programmer in
When you're not running three tiny
instances of Claude Code anymore. What if
you need 30?
Well, welcome to Gas Town, a new concept
by Steve Yegge. This is an example of
someone who isn't afraid to speculate on
what the future of software engineering
may look like. So, think of it as an
industrialized coding factory. You're
not managing agents, you're managing a
team. You describe features, you review
plans, you prioritize, you steer when
things go sideways, but you're not
writing code. You're not even reviewing
every line. 20 to 30 parallel agents,
that's impossible. So, Gas Town is a
take on coordinated teams of agents
with specialized roles.
And from Steve's post, huge disclaimer,
you probably shouldn't use it just yet.
And a few other valid reasons here.
However, it's a fascinating take on what
the future of agentic development may
look like.
So now I want to explore something that
many of us are processing.
How do we know if the agents are making
mistakes because if we have mistakes
we don't have reliable AI yet. You
might be thinking we do this by reading
code as code is the source of truth and
here is a great take from Charity
Majors.
You have never been able to know what
the code will do by reading the code.
You always needed to ask your
instrumentation to understand what your
code does in production.
When we give agents context, tools and
ability to check their own work, we will
have better results.
So when agents are generating code at
scale, static analysis and quality gates
like those from SonarQube become
essential guard rails in the phased
development workflow. Catching
structural issues before they compound.
So useful tools like this are great. And
notice that all of this requires a range
of things. Persistent task tracking,
coordination protocols, role
definitions, work order systems,
all the infrastructure we've been
talking about, externalized state,
explicit workflows. It's not optional at
this scale. It's the foundation.
And as you can see, it's a new
discipline. Without proper technique, we
can't blame AI for failing us. We just
need to learn it.
And the critical skill here is shaping
what goes into an agent to output
high-quality code. So context isn't just
our codebase. It's accumulated
architectural decisions, edge cases,
requirement clarifications, and
discovered constraints. It's also about
orchestration
in products or in businesses. Our domain
isn't always software. It's marketing.
It's finance. It's medicine and
validating the work product here isn't
as simple as reviewing the code. It
really depends on understanding complex
relationships between domain-specific
data. Only domain experts know what's
correct. And right now we're integrating
AI into their workflows with the same
approaches that barely work for coding.
Here's what most teams do when
integrating LLMs into products. They use
multi-agent architectures, orchestrators,
sub agents or role-based agents. These
agents call LLM APIs. They do some form
of RAG, maybe some convert text to SQL.
We influence model selection, temperature,
tool access, and then we do some kind of
post-facto evals where we adjust the
prompts. But let's be concrete about
what can go wrong with this approach.
First, the LLM call itself is a non-
deterministic step. Second, we have RAG.
And the naive RAG approach that people
use in products is fundamentally flawed.
It has no precision. If we ask it to
calculate something like the average
money spent on groceries per week or
find the most recent transaction amount,
semantic similarity fails here. It has
no temporal awareness. It's lossy and
it's prone to domain mismatch. Then we
have tools. Tools are way better for
deterministic calculations. But in
reality with dozens of tools there's no
guarantee that agent selects the right
one or orchestrates the right path. Then
we have text to SQL. It works for simple
translations but it can break down with
nuanced phrasing like "find ice cream
shops near my zip code with the names
that remind me of superheroes." That
logic can't directly translate to SQL.
And finally we have evals. We discover
opportunities after doing error analysis
on data from real users. That's
reactive.
But if we analyze the biggest failure
pattern across all production AI systems
serving business users is the fact that
success is determined by validation of
domain experts. So with coding agents,
we are the domain experts.
What happens when we apply AI to our
products? Our AI doesn't have
deterministic code for all scenarios.
There are exponentially more edge cases.
So for business tasks, having domain
expertise during logic formation is
critical for correctness, not just
evaluation afterwards. So how do we fix
it? We need to go one step further. We
can decouple planning from execution.
And here is the new mental model. First,
an LLM generates a comprehensive plan to
accomplish a task. This plan knows
everything about the underlying business
entities. Second, and this is the
critical step, domain experts can review
and refine the plan. And third, only
after the plan is approved, the system
generates an executable deterministic
artifact code that actually runs the
logic. This is a three-tier
architecture for context.
Tier one is the core data layer. This is
our semantic foundation and it is not
just text chunks and embeddings. It's
rich structural metadata. It includes
the entities, their definitions, the
temporal status of data, relationship
graphs, authority level, dependencies.
And this is how we close the gap between
retrieval and true structural
understanding. The Tier 2 is
orchestration layer. This is our team
collaboration zone. This is where your
domain experts operate. When the LLM
generates a plan, we can provide a way
for an expert to see it and see the reasoning
to refine it. They can say, "No, that's
wrong. The definition of a patient
record isn't capturing the new update
from the last month. Update the entity
graph to note that this patient record
is now inclusive of the new record
keeping system." They iterate on this
logic before any user sees it. And when
they're happy with it, they approve it
and it generates a deterministic
reusable and versioned artifact that
gets added to the product as an approved
functionality.
This is how the system can learn,
capture institutional knowledge and
scale the expert decisions, and SonarQube
can serve as a quality gate within
the orchestration layer validating
generated code artifacts for
reliability, security and
maintainability before they become
versioned deployed components.
And the Tier 3 here is the user
layer. This is how the results are
surfaced to the user. Once the
architecture beneath is solid, this part
is really straightforward.
This decoupled architecture changes the
role of our experts. We're not asking
them to be testers in a traditional
sense. We're asking them to onboard AI
properly with the right context,
validate that it works, and approve it,
making it shippable. So you give it
structured context, explain why
decisions are made, validate its
understanding before we ship to
production. You can choose the right
architecture for the right job and stick
to naive RAG for low stakes conceptual
tasks. Use advanced agentic RAG for
exploratory complex or ambiguous tasks.
And for high stakes deterministic
auditable tasks—finance, legal,
medicine, core product features—you
should really use the decoupled planning
and execution architecture. Here the
team validation is non-negotiable and
the result must be correct.
So let's bring it all together.
Architecting context for reliable AI is
a design problem with two core
components. First we must architect our
state. This is our persistent context
layer. In our coding example, this was
the evolution from simple chat history
to structured specs and finally to
graph-based issue tracking.
We externalized context into a
persistent version structure. In our
product example, this is the tier one
core data layer. We stopped using text
chunks with embeddings and built a full
persistent graph of rich structural
metadata definitive temporal relational
and descriptive enough to capture
entities and relationships in addition
to embeddings. And in both cases you
externalize what AI knows into a
versioned auditable system.
Second we must architect our logic. This
is our explicit orchestration layer. In
our coding example, it was our phased
development. We stopped reviewing
massive unreadable PRs and started
reviewing the plan before the code is
ever written. In our product example,
this was the Tier 2 team orchestration
layer. We decoupled planning from
execution and forced AI to produce
viewable plan that our team validates
before it becomes a deterministic
artifact that then can be verified with
tools like SonarQube. In both cases,
you externalize what the AI does into
reviewable auditable process.
So engineers are evolving from
individual contributors to something
entirely new. CTOs of agents.
Every engineer here is about to get
something CEOs wait decades for: a team
to lead. Not in 10 years, not after
climbing a career ladder. We're all
becoming technical executives with more
leverage than any generation before us.
So, if you think about it, from day one,
you're managing a team of highly
specialized, incredibly knowledgeable AI
agents. They are simultaneously more
capable and more flawed than any other
human team. They need direction,
validation, and most importantly,
someone who understands when they are
confidently wrong. You wake up in the
morning. Your monitoring agent has
flagged anomalies in last night's data
pipeline. Your diagnostic agent has run
the root cause and determined three
potential issues. Your code generation
agent prepared fixes for each scenario.
But here's where you come in. You need
to understand which fix aligns with
technical and business requirements,
which one maintains system integrity,
and which one won't cause cascading
failures
months from now.
So the career ladder is becoming a
career elevator, and you need to be
ready to jump to the floors that used to
take years to reach. So here's what this
means for you. The more capable AI
becomes, the more valuable deep
expertise becomes. It's
counterintuitive, but it's true. When
everyone can generate code, the person
who knows which code is correct becomes
most valuable. And the person who
understands why one approach will fail
at scale becomes the highest paid person
in the room.
Imagine walking into your work 5 years
from now. You run a team of AI agents
that amplify your capabilities 100x.
Your salary has doubled because you're
one of the few people who can guarantee
correct outcomes. You're not worried
about being replaced because you're the
one that's doing the replacing. You're
replacing chaos with control,
uncertainty with confidence, and
potential failures with proven success.
You're the expert who makes sure systems
work reliably, safely, profitably, and
you're the quality control that makes AI
trustworthy when companies deploy it at
scale. And when things go wrong, it's
not because AI is bad, but because
perfection is impossible, the real world
is messy and things change. We make
different tradeoffs and edge cases are
just infinite. So, you're here to make
AI deliver correct outcomes.
So, if we bring this full circle, don't
get distracted by the chaos. While
others may panic about AI replacing
everyone, you need to focus on becoming
the person in the room with the best
judgment, expertise, and best frameworks
for structural AI verification and
validation. I would love to continue
this conversation. Connect with me on
LinkedIn and I will share my slides with
you. Thank you.
